name: Patyk
classification_notes: Patyk is a display typeface rooted in pre- and post-WWII Polish handmade lettering, using unmodulated strokes that evoke a stick-sharpened tool; it sits outside conventional categories as a vernacular display face.
summary: A typeface by Marian Misiak drawn from Polish handmade poster lettering of the pre- and post-war era, translating the raw strokes of a roughly sharpened stick into a systematic digital typeface.
description: Patyk takes its name and character from the basic stick used by Polish sign-painters and poster-makers before and after World War II, a tool sharpened without a defined angle and producing flat, unmodulated strokes. Designer Marian Misiak has systematised that vernacular energy into a usable typeface, including alternates such as the crossed 'Y' found in Polish lettering of the pre-digital era. Available as a variable font when purchasing the full family.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe Patyk as rooted in a vernacular lettering tradition shaped by necessity, drawing from a popular pre- and post-WWII style used for handmade posters and everyday announcements. The typeface revisits a language of scarcity and immediacy, and includes alternate forms including a distinctive crossed 'Y' — a curious feature from Polish lettering before the digital era. Font design by Marian Misiak, font development by Nika Langosz.

name: Extract Grotesk
classification_notes: Described as a condensed grotesque with wooden type influence and a matching semi-slab variant; the core sans sits firmly in the grotesque tradition with a tactile, handmade quality.
summary: A slightly condensed grotesque with a matching semi-slab variant, originally commissioned for a Wrocław craft brewery and drawn from wooden type references.
description: Designed by Marian Misiak and Radek Łukasiewicz for Browar Stu Mostów, Extract pairs a clean condensed sans with a semi-slab version in which slab serifs appear selectively on capital letters. The slightly compressed proportions give it a bold, energetic rhythm suited to headlines and poster work, while the dual-family structure allows tonal shifts from modern and minimal to characterful and robust within a single coherent system. Its visual DNA draws from wooden grotesques, giving it a tactile quality that sits well in craft and independent brand contexts.
foundry_blurb: Extract blends a sturdy Sans with a Semi-Slab variant where slab serifs subtly enrich the capital letters, originally created for Browar Stu Mostów, a craft brewery in Wrocław, Poland. Its slightly condensed proportions — the source of its name — give it a bold yet agile rhythm, and its stylistic DNA draws from wooden grotesques, carrying a tactile, handmade energy suited to experimental and characterful design work.

name: Extract Semislab
classification_notes: Classified as a semi-slab because slab serifs appear selectively on capital letters only, blending a sans grotesque base with partial serif detailing rather than a full slab treatment. The condensed proportions and wooden grotesk references reinforce the slab-adjacent grotesque classification.
summary: A semi-slab grotesque with slightly condensed proportions, designed for Browar Stu Mostów and drawing on wooden grotesk references.
description: Originally created for a craft brewery in Wrocław, this typeface pairs a sans grotesque base with slab serifs restricted to capital letters, giving designers a tonal range between clean and characterful without switching families. The condensed proportions are baked into its name and form, producing a bold but agile rhythm suited to headlines, posters and expressive layouts. Its wooden grotesk DNA gives it a tactile, handmade quality that extends its appeal beyond the hospitality sector.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe Extract as a dual-structure family pairing a sturdy Sans with a Semi-Slab variant, where capital letters gain subtle slab serifs. Conceived for Browar Stu Mostów in Wrocław, the design draws on wooden grotesks and the experimental spirit of craft brewing culture, with slightly condensed proportions reflected in the typeface's name. Creative direction and design by Marian Misiak, with production by Radek Łukasiewicz.

name: Tor grotesk
classification_notes: Follows the geometric, industrial doctrine of DIN-style typefaces, developed originally for functional and standardisation purposes, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif category with a strong DIN lineage.
summary: A DIN-influenced geometric sans drawn for a book about radical Polish train enthusiasts, featuring heavily slanted italics and a variable font axis controlling the italic angle.
description: Rooted in the geometric, functional doctrine of DIN typography, Tor grotesk was purpose-built for Kult Jednostki/Radical Passion, a 500-page book documenting Poland's electric train subculture published by Torypress. Its most distinctive feature is a heavily slanted italic, available as a variable font where the angle of inclination can be adjusted across a continuous axis. The design takes visual cues from mid-twentieth century Polish railway standardisation norms and historical train lettering specimens.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe Tor grotesk as arriving from railway tracks and train depots, following the strict geometric doctrine of DIN's industrial typographic family. It was primarily designed for a book about radical train enthusiasts and the world's longest-produced electric train, a project involving four years of travel, interviews with spotters, graffiti writers, model makers and train drivers, drawing on private and long-forgotten archives.

name: Maria Connected & Unconnected
classification_notes: The letterforms are derived from FIDU technology, an air-pressure metal-forming process, giving them a three-dimensional, inflated quality that places them firmly in the experimental display category rather than any conventional type classification.
summary: A display typeface by Oskar Zięta whose letterforms are derived from FIDU technology, the same air-pressure metal-forming process behind his celebrated PLOPP stool.
description: Maria exists in two variants, Connected and Unconnected, with letterforms shaped by FIDU technology, an experimental process for forming metal objects using air pressure, patented by Zięta. The typeface extends into physical metal letters available for purchase, and the family includes dozens of arrows and pictograms designed specifically for wayfinding applications. The crossover between industrial manufacturing process and type design gives Maria a sculptural, inflated character unlike conventionally drawn display faces.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe Maria as more than a typeface, noting that its letterforms are shaped using FIDU technology developed by designer Oskar Zięta, the same patented process behind the PLOPP air-inflated steel stool. The typeface is accompanied by physical metal letters available for purchase, and includes a comprehensive set of arrows and pictograms intended for wayfinding systems.

name: New Zelek Pro
classification_notes: Originally designed as a dry-transfer lettering for Mecanorma in 1974 by a leading Polish poster artist, the design sits firmly in the display category with expressive, decorative letterforms rooted in mid-century analogue lettering traditions.
summary: A digital revival of Bronisław Zelek's 1974 Mecanorma dry-transfer lettering, produced in consultation with its author and expanded with Cyrillic, extensive kerning and connected diacritic variants.
description: Originally released as a dry-transfer typeface for the French company Mecanorma in 1974, New Zelek became the most recognisable typeface of communist-era Polish advertising and found its way onto international music album covers. The Pro revival was consulted directly with Zelek, a leading figure of the Polish School of Posters, and brings over 9,000 kerning pairs, additional characters absent from the analogue original, and connected and unconnected variants for creative handling of diacritical marks. Latin and Cyrillic scripts are included.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe New Zelek Pro as the return of the most popular Polish analogue-era typeface, originally conceived as a Mecanorma dry transfer in 1974 and now digitally adapted in consultation with its author, Bronisław Zelek. The revival includes Latin and Cyrillic characters, more than 9,000 kerning pairs, and connected and unconnected letter variants to handle diacritics creatively.

name: Sudety
classification_notes: The page offers minimal technical detail, but the narrative framing around a specific place and community suggests a distinctive display typeface rather than a workhorse text family.
summary: A display typeface by Jan Estrada-Osmycki, designed in the Sudety mountain village of Sokołowsko, a place known for underground culture and slow living.
description: Designed by Jan Estrada-Osmycki during a stay in Sokołowsko, a former health resort in south-western Poland with a reputation for underground music and arthouse cinema. The typeface takes its name and spirit from the Sudety mountain range that spans eastern Germany, Poland and the Czech Republic. Its origin story leans into a particular mood of analogue retreat and cultural specificity.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe Sudety as a font designed in Sokołowsko, a village in the Sudety mountains once known as 'Polish Davos', now a gathering point for underground music fans and slow-life enthusiasts. The typeface was designed by Jan Estrada-Osmycki, who is half Polish and half Mexican, with illustrations also by his hand.

name: Tor Grotesk Ceramic Set
classification_notes: This page describes a physical ceramic product set, not a typeface. It uses Tor Grotesk as a design element but is not itself a font release. No meaningful typographic classification applies.
summary: A limited-run set of three handmade ceramic objects designed by Threedotstype, using typographic point sizes as the sizing system.
description: This is a physical product rather than a typeface: a set comprising a handleless mug (125pt.), an espresso cup (102pt.) and a candle holder (76pt.), each made by hand and assembled by the Threedotstype team. The concept translates traditional type specimen aesthetics into ceramic form, treating typographic point sizes as a shared dimensional code. Graphic design and concept by Marian Misiak; Tor Grotesk is used as the display typeface throughout.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe the Tor Grotesk Ceramic Set as an experiment in applying typographic point sizes to physical objects, translating type specimen aesthetics into handmade ceramics. Each set of three objects was carefully assembled by their team, with thanks to Zakwas Studio in Poland for their help in the process.

name: HAHAHA / AHAHAH
classification_notes: Rubin Sans is described as a display typography font, drawn with a ruler and draftsman curve, placing it firmly in the display category with grotesque influences given its sans-serif structure and artistic origins.
summary: A type specimen booklet documenting Rubin Sans, a display sans drawn from the art practice of Polish artist Honza Zamojski across nearly a decade of drawings, murals and sculptures.
description: HAHAHA / AHAHAH is a 64-page printed specimen for Rubin Sans, a display font developed by Honza Zamojski from letters originally sketched with a ruler and draftsman curve for paintings, murals and art books. The booklet is split between a yellow section of chronological texts from Zamojski's artworks and a black-and-white photographic section, all set in Rubin Sans and Di Grotesk. Published by Threedotstype at 23 x 12.5cm, it functions as both an artist's monograph and a typographic record of the font's evolution from 2014 to 2023.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe HAHAHA / AHAHAH as a publication tracing Rubin Sans from art practice to typography, presenting Honza Zamojski's texts and artworks in chronological order between 2014 and 2023. The 64-page booklet is divided into a yellow section of quotes and a black-and-white section of artwork photographs, all typeset in Rubin Sans and Di Grotesk, designed by Marian Misiak and Honza Zamojski and printed on Creamy HiBulk paper in Pantone Yellow and black.

name: This way. Guide
classification_notes: This product is a printed exhibition guide rather than a typeface; it does not fit neatly into any typeface category. Classified as display by necessity of the schema, though it is more accurately a typographic publication.
summary: A 60-page printed exhibition guide exploring the history and cultural significance of arrows and navigation symbols, produced by Threedotstype for the Weltformat Graphic Design Festival in 2019.
description: Published alongside a type specimen focused on arrows, this guide traces the origins of navigation symbols from prehistoric pointing devices to digital cursors, touching on everything from brothel signage to Cupid's iconography. Printed in two Pantone colours on lightweight Stora Enso creamy HiBulk paper at 23 x 12.5 cm, the format was chosen deliberately with international shipping in mind. It is a physical companion to Threedotstype's broader obsession with directional symbols, which inform the navigation glyph sets found across all their typefaces.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe their obsession with arrows as central to their work as a type foundry, with all their fonts containing additional navigation symbol sets. The 'This Way!' initiative grew out of that obsession into two publications: a type specimen and this exhibition guide, both exploring the historical and cultural background of arrows and wayfinding tools. The guide was created for an exhibition at the Weltformat Graphic Design Festival in Luzern, Switzerland in 2019.

name: Variable Radius
classification_notes: Radius is a display typeface built around a variable font axis system controlling top, bottom and width parameters, designed specifically to morph characters when set on circular or curved baselines.
summary: A variable display typeface by Radek Łukasiewicz with three axes controlling top, bottom and width parameters, optimised for setting type on circular paths.
description: Radius is a variable font with three axes, top, bottom and width, that allow characters to morph across a wide range of contours. While it functions on a conventional baseline, its design intent is circular setting, where the variable axes adjust the anatomy of each character to follow curved shapes. This listing is for the physical specimen booklet, a 64-page pocket dictionary of English, French and Polish circle-related proverbs printed on fluorescent and Munken white papers.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe the Variable Radius specimen as a celebration of printed matter and a showcase for the Radius typeface, taking the form of a pocket English–French–Polish dictionary of circle-related proverbs. The 64-page booklet, printed on fluorescent and high-quality white papers, questions the role of a physical specimen in a digital age, while demonstrating how Radius's three variable axes allow characters to adapt when typeset on round shapes.

name: Support Independent Type II
classification_notes: This page does not describe a typeface but rather a printed book about independent type design; no typographic classification is applicable.
summary: A printed anthology of type specimens from over 300 independent type foundries, published as a follow-up to the 2020 edition.
description: This is not a typeface release but a 288-page softcover book published by Slanted Publishers, edited by Lars Harmsen, Marian Misiak and Pola Małaczewska. It collects specimens from more than 300 independent type foundries alongside commentary on current trends in type design, printed on uncoated and transparent papers with offset spot-colour printing. The book positions independent foundries as a cultural and commercial counterpoint to corporate font distributors.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describes Support Independent Type II as the highly anticipated follow-up to the acclaimed 2020 book, celebrating the culture of independent type specimens and their influence on design and typography. Featuring over 300 cutting-edge type foundries, the book spans digital formats, printed works and tactile type objects, exploring how independent type design reflects cultural shifts and sparks creative innovation. It is presented as both a manifesto and a visual art object, essential reading for graphic designers, creative directors and anyone passionate about type.

name: Maria letters
classification_notes: Maria letters are physical blown metal characters designed for wayfinding and interior decoration, placing them firmly in the display/decorative category with no conventional digital type classification applicable.
summary: Physical blown metal alphabet letters available in connected and unconnected variants, designed for wayfinding signage and interior decoration.
description: Produced from blown metal on request, these 300 mm characters come in Inox and RAL 7021 dark grey matt finishes, each supplied with a mounting kit ready for wall installation. The forms are deliberately curvy and visible, positioned against minimal no-design aesthetics, and suited to wayfinding applications such as car parks as well as decorative domestic use. Average production time is five weeks with free shipping included.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype pitch Maria letters as an antidote to anonymous design, offering visible, readable and curvy characters for wayfinding and decoration. Available in connected and unconnected versions, the blown metal letters come in Inox or RAL 7021 dark grey matt, mount directly to walls via an included kit, and ship free within approximately five weeks of ordering.

name: This Way. Specimen
classification_notes: This is a physical type specimen publication rather than a typeface itself; it showcases arrow symbols across Threedotstype's font catalogue and was produced in conjunction with an exhibition at Weltformat 2019.
summary: A printed type specimen from Threedotstype focused on arrows and navigation symbols found across their entire font catalogue, produced for the Weltformat Graphic Design Festival in 2019.
description: An 80-page booklet printed in two Pantone colours on lightweight Creamy HiBulk 53g paper, the publication explores the historical and cultural background of arrows alongside showcasing the navigation symbol sets embedded in every Threedotstype font. It accompanied an exhibition at the Weltformat Graphic Design Festival in Luzern, Switzerland in 2019. The specimen is now out of stock.
foundry_blurb: Threedotstype describe their obsession with arrows as the driving force behind this publication, noting that all their fonts contain additional sets of navigation symbols. The specimen pairs typographic showcases with broader historical storytelling about arrows, from prehistoric wayfinding to Cupid's attribute, and was produced on sustainably made lightweight paper designed to travel. It also served as the guide for an exhibition shown at Weltformat 2019 in Luzern.
